text: A sixty-eight-year-old man was recently admitted with a small bowel obstruction in the context of a previous gastro-jejunostomy for a duodenal stricture. This was managed with a small bowel resection and adhesionolysis. He then presented one week post-discharge with an acute abdomen and hemodynamic instability. A CT scan showed a moderate-sized collection in the left upper quadrant in free air.

During the urgent exploratory laparotomy, dense adhesions are visible throughout the abdomen. A sump drain is inserted into the collection in the left upper quadrant, and the procedure is aborted due to the frozen abdomen.

The CT scan images show a leak in the gastric fundus, with extravasation visible and marked by green arrows on sagittal and coronal views. During retroflexion, the perforation in the fundus is observed, followed by an attempt at endoscopic closure.

Using a double channel gastroscope, the surgeon advances a polyloop through one channel and a hemostatic clip through the other. The polyloop is positioned between the prongs of the clip, which is deployed on one side of the gastric defect. A second clip is placed on the opposite side, with the polyloop similarly positioned. The polyloop is then tightened and deployed, effectively closing the defect akin to an interrupted suture.

The surgeon uses a loop cutter to trim the polyloop tail, ensuring a clear working space. This process is repeated until the first defect is fully closed. A second perforation distal to the first is identified and closed using the same technique.
The surgeon examines both repaired perforations closely, confirming complete closure. A follow-up CT scan with oral contrast shows the repaired areas, highlighted by green arrows.
